name: Naturepedia Climate Carbon Feedbacks Knowledge Mesh
description: Machine-readable knowledge mesh connecting Climate Carbon Feedbacks™ to Earth Systems™, Carbon Cycle™, Forest Carbon Systems™, Soil Carbon Systems™, Carbon & Microbial Life™, Water Systems™, Plant Intelligence™, and Ecosystem Feedbacks™.
architecture: Earth Systems → Carbon Cycle → Forest Carbon Systems → Soil Carbon Systems → Carbon & Microbial Life → Water Systems → Plant Intelligence → Ecosystem Feedbacks → Climate Carbon Feedbacks

relationshipMesh.relationship: Climate carbon feedbacks operate across Earth systems by linking atmosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, soils, forests, microbes, water movement, and planetary energy balance.
The carbon cycle regulates atmospheric carbon through photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion, ocean exchange, soil carbon storage, forest carbon storage, and long-term sequestration.
Atmospheric carbon dioxide and methane influence radiative forcing by changing how heat is retained, released, amplified, or buffered within the climate system.
Forest carbon systems create climate feedbacks through canopy cooling, carbon sequestration, fire risk, drought stress, tree mortality, regrowth, deadwood decomposition, and old-growth storage.
Soil carbon systems influence climate feedbacks through carbon stabilization, soil respiration, erosion, moisture protection, thawing soils, microbial activity, and regenerative soil carbon recovery.
Microbial communities affect climate feedbacks through decomposition, respiration, methane cycling, nutrient transformation, microbial biomass, necromass, and warming-sensitive carbon release.
Water systems shape climate resilience through soil moisture, wetlands, snowpack, floodplains, groundwater, evapotranspiration, drought buffering, and carbon-storage stability.
Plants regulate carbon sinks through photosynthesis, root carbon allocation, stress response, nutrient sensing, mycorrhizal partnerships, water-use decisions, and long-term biomass formation.
Positive climate feedbacks emerge when warming accelerates carbon release through fire, drought, decomposition, soil respiration, thaw, forest mortality, wetland methane, and reduced carbon storage.
Negative climate feedbacks stabilize systems when forests, soils, wetlands, plants, microbes, and water cycles increase carbon storage, cooling, biodiversity, recovery, or resilience.

Climate carbon feedbacks become Earth system feedbacks when carbon sinks, carbon sources, radiative forcing, water cycles, plant growth, forest health, soil carbon, and microbial activity interact at planetary scale.
